header.subtitle: Labour vulnerability to AI · Spain 2026 · 502 CNO-11 occupations · LFS Q4 2025 · Census 2021
header.title: Job Vulnerability to Artificial Intelligence in Spain
header.description: Analysis of 502 occupations in the Spanish labour market by their degree of AI exposure. Data INE/LFS Q4 2025 · Census 2021 weights · Scores calibrated with 5 Spain-specific structural factors (DESI 2023, labour protection, EU AI Act Annex III, AESIA).

disclaimer.title: ⚠ Note on employment data:
disclaimer.text: The LFS only publishes employment at major group level (1-digit CNO). The 4-digit figures have been proportionally distributed within each 3-digit subgroup, weighted with INE Census 2021 data (145 subgroups). These are not individually observed occupation-level figures.
disclaimer.italic: Proportional estimate — not observed at 4-digit level.

scatter.bubbleNote: Bubble size represents the occupation's share of total employment.

detail.weightedIndex: Weighted index, not wage bill at risk
detail.reliabilityText: Score validated inter-model (55% coverage, 277/502 occupations). κ<sub>w</sub> = 0.667 (substantial agreement). MAD = 1.0 point.
detail.shareText: {{name}} (CNO {{cno}}) · AI Exposure: {{score}}/10 · {{employees}} employees · Average salary: {{salary}} · Wage bill × exposure index: {{index}} · Impact type: {{impact}} · EU AI Act: {{risk}}

tooltip.clickToInspect: Click to inspect {{count}} occupations in this sector

sensitivity.title: Sensitivity analysis: ±20% on calibration factors
sensitivity.description: The 5 calibration factors are expert judgement without empirical back-testing. This table shows how key figures change when each factor is shifted ±20%. The multiplicative formula compounds the uncertainty.
sensitivity.note: Note: "High exposure" figures are not linearly recalculated because they depend on individual thresholds per occupation. The wage bill × exposure index scales proportionally.

methodology.title: Methodology:
methodology.text1: 502 CNO-11 occupations (INE/SEPE). Employment: LFS Q4 2025 microdata (22.46M employed), distributed using structural weights from the 2021 Population Census (INE, administrative records, 145 subgroups at 3-digit CNO).
methodology.text2: Salaries: Wage Structure Survey 2023 (INE), adjusted with INE education premia and international proxies (INSEE France, INE-PT Portugal). 128 unique values.
methodology.text3: AI exposure scores (0-10) calibrated with 5 Spain-specific structural factors: (1) DESI 2023 digitalisation index by sector (69.8 aggregate points, 3rd EU — EC no longer publishes composite DESI post-2023), (2) services sector weight, (3) labour protection (Workers' Statute), (4) AI use-case classification per EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689, Annex III — classifies AI systems by use case, not occupations), (5) AESIA supervision (Royal Decree 729/2023).
methodology.wageIndexText: Calculated as Employment × Salary × (Score/10). A weighted index reflecting the concentration of wage bill in high-exposure occupations. It does not represent wage bill "at risk" or "threatened" — a high score indicates theoretical exposure, not displacement.
methodology.employmentText: 4-digit CNO figures are proportional estimates (LFS→1-digit, redistributed with Census 2021 weights to 3 digits). These are not individually observed figures. The LFS includes ~3.3M self-employed; the Wage Structure Survey excludes them by design.
methodology.internationalText: The OECD estimates 28% of jobs at risk of automation in Spain, but that figure includes all technologies (robotics, traditional software, AI). The OECD AI-specific figure for Spain is 5.9% high risk (traditional automation) or 27.4% exposure to generative AI (different construct). Our 20.2% measures theoretical AI exposure calibrated with Spain-specific structural factors.
methodology.llmText: Scores were generated with Gemini 2.5 Pro (T=0.2, structured prompts) and validated through blind re-scoring of 100 stratified occupations by GPT-4o, plus sub-component re-scoring (GPT 5.4 + Gemini 3) of 177 additional occupations (7.0 and 5.0 clusters). Inter-model validation coverage: 55% (277/502). Holistic results: r = 0.715 (good), ICC(2,1) = 0.701 (good), κ<sub>w</sub> = 0.667 (substantial agreement, Landis-Koch). 84% of occupations agree within ±2.0 points. 5.0 re-scoring: 90% agreement within ±1.0, median diff 0.42.
methodology.noteText: Scores are theoretical exposure estimates, not predictions of job displacement. Empirical evidence (Anthropic, February 2026) shows that theoretical exposure can differ significantly from actual adoption (94% theoretical vs 33% observed in computing/mathematical occupations). Full methodology with 44 technical notes available in PDF.

footer.credit: Methodology V23 · Inter-model validation: κw = 0.667 · © 2026 A. de Nicolás, M. Sureda
